title: Indications and Interpretation
narrationScript: Finding particles on a part is the easy half of magnetic particle testing. Deciding what they mean is the half that earns your certification. In this lesson we work through everything that stands between a cluster of particles and a correct call. The words matter, so let us set them first. An indication is anything that attracts and holds particles. Interpretation is deciding what caused the indication. Evaluation, which we cover in the next lesson, is deciding what that cause means against the acceptance criteria. Before you judge a single indication, know why the test exists. A brand new forging being screened at a mill, a pump shaft at overhaul, and a crane hook in a failure investigation may carry the same steel, but they are different tests. The purpose sets the coverage, the criteria, and how hard you look. Component use and condition matter the same way. A part with service history tells you where it has been loaded, where it has been repaired, and where fatigue likes to start. Surface condition decides what you can see at all. Scale, paint, and heavy coatings mute leakage fields, and a thin coating is acceptable only when your procedure has demonstrated detection through it. Now the human factors, because the instrument that reads the indication is you. Your eyes are checked on a schedule, typically a near vision test every year plus a color and contrast check, because a technician who cannot resolve fine detail cannot find fine cracks. Fatigue, glare, awkward access, and production pressure all measurably lower detection. Expectation bias is the sneakiest enemy. A part that always passes trains you to see a pass. Inspect every part like it is the one that will fail. Lighting has hard numbers, and you should know them cold. For visible particles, white light at the inspection surface of at least one hundred footcandles, which is roughly one thousand lux. For fluorescent particles, ultraviolet intensity at the surface of at least one thousand microwatts per square centimeter, with ambient white light held to two footcandles or less, which is about twenty lux. Those values are measured with calibrated meters at the surface, not assumed, and they are rechecked on a schedule, typically every shift and after every bulb or filter change. Give your eyes at least one minute to adapt when you enter the darkened booth, and many procedures call for several. Do not wear photochromic lenses under ultraviolet light, because they darken exactly when you need to see. And respect the lamp. Never stare into it, keep skin exposure reasonable, and pull any lamp with a cracked filter out of service immediately. Continuity of inspection means the test is systematic, not casual. Scan in an overlapping pattern. Finish a zone before you break. Cover every surface with two roughly perpendicular field directions before you call the part done. Keep the application to viewing rhythm of the continuous method, and never clean or demagnetize a part before its results are recorded. Then comes the call. A false indication is held by something other than a leakage field. Particles drained into a groove, powder caught on rough scale, or the heavy background fur of an over magnetized part. Clean it up, fix the technique, run it again. A nonrelevant indication comes from a real leakage field with a benign cause. Abrupt section changes, internal drilled holes near the surface, keyways, the permeability boundary at a weld fusion line, or the scribble of magnetic writing from contact with another magnetized part. The defenses are simple. Wipe and reapply to test reproducibility. Demagnetize and retest for magnetic writing. Check the drawing for geometry. A relevant indication is caused by an actual discontinuity, and only relevant indications move on to evaluation. The last skill is depth. A sharp, dense, tightly held line means a surface break, usually a crack or a seam. A broad, fuzzy, weak pattern means the flaw is below the surface, and the deeper it sits the fuzzier it gets. Alternating current sees the skin only. Rectified current with dry powder reaches deepest, but even then magnetic particle testing is a near surface method. Read the pattern, name the cause, and you are ready to evaluate.
imagePrompt: In a darkened inspection booth, a technician under a handheld UV lamp leans close to a steel weldment as bright green-yellow fluorescent indications glow along the weld toe.

sections.heading: Indication, Interpretation, Evaluation
sections.body:
Three words carry precise meanings in this method, and mixing them up causes real trouble. An **indication** is any accumulation of particles. **Interpretation** decides what caused it — a discontinuity, the part's geometry, or the technique itself. **Evaluation** (next lesson) compares a relevant indication against acceptance criteria. This lesson is about interpretation, and about the conditions that make interpretation trustworthy.

Interpretation starts before the first particle, with two questions. First, the **purpose of the test**: screening new forgings at a mill, recertifying a pump shaft at overhaul, and dissecting a failure each demand different coverage, different criteria, and a different level of suspicion. Second, **component use and condition**: a part's service history tells you where load concentrates and where fatigue starts — fillets, threads, keyways, weld toes — and its surface condition limits what you can see. Scale, heavy paint, and thick coatings mute the leakage field; a thin nonconductive coating is tolerable only where the procedure has **demonstrated** detection through it. Prior weld repairs deserve special attention: they change local permeability, and they mark the places where the part has already had trouble. Know why you are testing and what the part has lived through, and the indications start making sense before you interpret a single one.

sections.heading: Human Factors
sections.body:
The reading instrument is the technician. Certification programs require a **vision program** — typically a near-vision acuity check (Jaeger-type chart) every year plus a color and contrast differentiation check — because nobody finds fine indications with eyes that cannot resolve them. Beyond the paperwork: **fatigue** measurably lowers detection; so does **production pressure** that rushes the scan, and so does awkward access that turns inspection into gymnastics. **Expectation bias** is the quiet killer — a part number that always passes trains your brain to see a pass; inspect every part as if it is the one that fails.

In fluorescent work, give your eyes time: at least **one minute of dark adaptation** when you enter the booth, and many procedures specify several minutes. Never wear **photochromic** (self-darkening) lenses under UV-A — they darken precisely when you need vision most. Take short breaks on long runs, refocus your eyes at distance, and remember that the last hour of a shift is statistically the most dangerous one for a miss.

sections.heading: Lighting: The Numbers That Make a Test Valid
sections.body:
Lighting requirements are conditions for a valid test, not suggestions.

- **Visible particles:** at least **100 footcandles (about 1,000 lux)** of white light at the examination surface.
- **Fluorescent particles:** UV-A intensity of at least **1,000 µW/cm² at the surface**, with ambient white light at **2 fc (about 20 lux) or less** in the viewing area.

Both are verified with **calibrated meters at the surface** — a radiometer for UV-A, a white-light meter for ambient and visible work — and the readings are recorded. Intensity falls off quickly with distance, so the check is made at the working distance; many shops standardize the lamp-to-surface distance (a 15-inch convention is common) so readings are comparable. Re-check on a schedule — typically **each shift or every 8 hours**, after every bulb or filter change, and whenever the setup moves. Mercury-vapor lamps need a **warm-up** period before they reach full output; LED UV-A sources must be qualified per the governing practice and checked the same way. A booth with a white-light leak above the ambient limit fails the test conditions no matter how strong the UV is — find the leak before you inspect.

sections.title: UV-A discipline
sections.body: Never look into a UV-A source or sweep it across anyone's eyes. Pull any lamp with a **cracked or missing filter** out of service immediately — the filter is what blocks the harsher short-wavelength UV. Cover skin for prolonged close work, and do not wear photochromic lenses under UV — they darken when you need to see. UV protection rules come from your safety program; the inspection numbers come from your procedure. Both are mandatory.

sections.caption: A compliant fluorescent viewing station: UV-A intensity verified at the surface, ambient white light held down, and adaptation time respected.

sections.heading: View Conditions and Continuity of Inspection
sections.body:
**View conditions** are the geometry of seeing: get close enough to resolve fine detail (generally within arm's length of the surface), keep your viewing angle reasonably square to the surface, and position the part or yourself so every zone can actually be seen — mirrors and good housekeeping beat guesswork on bores and back sides. The surface itself is part of the view: drips, fog on lenses, and excess bath all hide indications.

**Continuity of inspection** means the examination is systematic, not casual:

- Scan in an **overlapping pattern** so no strip of surface is skipped.
- Finish the zone you are scanning before any interruption, and re-adapt your eyes after any exposure to white light.
- Cover every required surface in **two roughly perpendicular field directions** before the part is called complete.
- Keep the continuous-method rhythm — bath on, bath diverted, current on, then look — identical every cycle.
- Never demagnetize or postclean before the results are interpreted and recorded; on large weldments, mark off inspected zones as you go.

sections.caption: Four particle patterns and what they mean: surface crack, subsurface flaw, geometry leakage, and a false pattern with no leakage field at all.

sections.heading: Relevant, Nonrelevant, or False
sections.body:
Interpretation sorts every indication into one of three bins.

**False indications** are held by something other than a magnetic leakage field: particles drained into a groove or recess, powder mechanically caught on rough surface or scale, or the heavy background **furring** of an over-magnetized part (excess amperage pulls particles to the surface everywhere, especially at edges). The fix is technique: clean the surface, re-verify the amperage against the qualified value, and run it again.

**Nonrelevant indications** come from real leakage fields with benign causes — the part's design or structure, not a discontinuity: abrupt **section changes**, internal **drilled holes** or keyways near the surface, press-fit and shrink-fit lines, the **permeability boundary** between weld metal and base metal, external poles at part ends, and **magnetic writing** — the loose scribble left where the part touched another magnetized part. Confirm the cause before you dismiss it: wipe the area and re-apply with current to test reproducibility; for suspected magnetic writing, demagnetize and re-test (writing disappears, a flaw comes back); for geometry, check the drawing — the indication should sit exactly at the feature and repeat on every identical part.

**Relevant indications** are caused by actual discontinuities. Only these move forward to evaluation against acceptance criteria.

sections.heading: Surface or Subsurface
sections.body:
Once an indication is relevant, its **shape testifies to its depth**. A **surface-breaking** discontinuity — crack, seam, lap reaching the surface — interrupts flux right at the skin, so the leakage field is strong and concentrated: the indication is **sharp, dense, narrow, and tightly held**, hard to wipe away. A **subsurface** discontinuity leaks a weaker, wider field through the covering metal: the indication is **broad, fuzzy, weak, and loosely held**, and it gets fuzzier the deeper the flaw sits.

Current type sets what is even possible. **AC** concentrates flux at the surface (skin effect) — superb particle mobility for surface flaws, nearly blind below. **HWDC and FWDC** drive flux deeper; HWDC with dry powder is the classic combination for near-surface flaws in welds and rough work. Even so, be honest about the physics: MT is a **surface and near-surface** method — useful subsurface response fades within roughly a quarter inch even under favorable DC-type conditions. A broad fuzzy indication tells you something is down there; it does not size it or measure depth. When the question becomes how big and how deep, the answer belongs to volumetric methods like UT or RT, and your report should say so.

interactive.title: Relevant, Nonrelevant, or False?
interactive.intro: Classify each indication the way you would on the bench.

items.label: Sharp, dense line in a shaft fillet that returns after wipe and re-test
items.why: A reproducible leakage-field indication at a stress riser is a probable fatigue crack — interpret it as relevant and move to evaluation.

items.label: Broad, fuzzy patch on a forging at the qualified amperage with HWDC
items.why: Weak, diffuse buildup is the classic signature of a subsurface discontinuity — still relevant, still evaluated.

items.label: Even particle band ringing an abrupt shoulder diameter change
items.why: Leakage at a section change is caused by part geometry, not a discontinuity — confirm against the drawing and note it.

items.label: Loose squiggle that vanishes after demagnetizing and re-testing
items.why: Magnetic writing from contact with another magnetized part: a real leakage field, but no defect — it does not return after demag.

items.label: Faint straight line tracing a weld fusion-zone boundary
items.why: The permeability difference between weld metal and base metal holds particles without any flaw present.

items.label: Heavy overall fur of particles after the amperage was doubled
items.why: Over-magnetization furring — excessive background everywhere, not a leakage field from a flaw. Re-verify the amperage and re-run.

items.label: Streak of particles running down a drain groove on a wet part
items.why: A drainage pattern: gravity put the particles there, not magnetism.

items.label: Powder held in a band of rough mill scale
items.why: Mechanical trapping on a rough surface — clean to bare metal and re-examine.

checkQuestions.question: For fluorescent MT, typical procedure values require the examination conditions to be at least:
options: 1,000 µW/cm² UV-A at the surface, with ambient white light at 2 fc or less
100 µW/cm² UV-A at the surface, with ambient white light at 20 fc or less
2 fc of white light at the surface with any UV level
1,000 µW/cm² UV-A measured at the bulb face, ambient light unrestricted
checkQuestions.explanation: Fluorescent examination needs at least about 1,000 microwatts per square centimeter of UV-A at the surface and a darkened viewing area at or below about 2 footcandles, both verified with calibrated meters.

checkQuestions.question: Which statement correctly pairs an indication's appearance with its depth?
options: Sharp and dense means subsurface; broad and fuzzy means surface
Sharp, dense, tightly held means a surface break; broad, fuzzy, weak means subsurface
Both patterns indicate over-magnetization
Fuzzy patterns are always false indications
checkQuestions.explanation: A surface break interrupts flux at the skin and produces a strong, concentrated leakage field; a subsurface flaw leaks a weaker, wider field through the covering metal, so its indication is broad and fuzzy.

checkQuestions.question: A uniform particle band rings the shoulder where a shaft steps down in diameter, in the same place on every shaft in the batch. The correct interpretation is:
options: Reject every shaft — any indication is cause for rejection
Nonrelevant — leakage from the section change; verify against the drawing and note it
False — particles cannot collect at geometry features
Increase the amperage until the band disappears
checkQuestions.explanation: An abrupt section change forces real leakage flux out of the part, holding particles with no discontinuity present. Confirming it sits exactly at the geometric feature, on every part, makes it nonrelevant.

checkQuestions.question: You suspect a loose, irregular squiggle of particles is magnetic writing. The confirming move is to:
options: Demagnetize and re-test — magnetic writing does not return; a discontinuity indication does
Reject the part immediately
Double the bath concentration and look again
Switch from continuous to residual method
checkQuestions.explanation: Magnetic writing is a stray residual pattern from contact with another magnetized part. Demagnetizing erases it; a real flaw re-indicates when the part is re-magnetized.

checkQuestions.question: Which set of habits maintains continuity of inspection?
options: Random spot checks across the part to save time
Overlapping systematic scanning, finishing each zone before breaks, and covering all surfaces in two roughly perpendicular field directions before release
A single field direction at higher amperage
Demagnetizing and postcleaning before interpreting, for a cleaner view
checkQuestions.explanation: Continuity means systematic, complete coverage: overlapped scan paths, finished zones, both field directions, and no cleanup until results are interpreted and recorded.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §12.1–12.6 (human factors, view conditions, continuity, lighting, component use, purpose)
CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §12.7 (interpretation: relevant vs. nonrelevant, surface vs. subsurface)
Lighting and viewing practice in standards such as ASTM E709 and ASTM E1444 (generic reference)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 examination conditions (generic reference)
